title: Lipstick on Cotton
stainChemistry: Classification: Oil/Grease (lipid-based). Lipstick is one of the most complex cosmetic stains: it contains wax (beeswax, carnauba, candelilla — provides structure), oils (castor oil, lanolin, mineral oil — provides glide), and concentrated dye (D&C Red 6, Red 7, Red 21 — organic fluorescent dyes plus iron oxide pigments). The wax component solidifies on cotton and traps oil and dye within its matrix. Oil stains penetrate fiber structure through capillary action and hydrophobic affinity. Treatment: dry-side solvent (POG) to dissolve lipid phase, followed by surfactant emulsification to remove residual oil.
whyThisWorks: Cosmetics contain oils, waxes, and pigments — the oil spreads the color deeper. Cotton absorbs quickly and holds stains deep in the weave. Dish soap or a degreaser breaks down the oil so it becomes water-soluble and can be rinsed away.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply POG directly to the oil stain. POG is a volatile dry solvent with strong affinity for lipids — it dissolves oil droplets on contact. Tamp gently and blot with absorbent cloth. Dwell 5–10 minutes.

2. If POG plateaus, apply amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. Amyl acetate is especially effective on older or polymerized oil stains. Blot with clean absorbent cloth between applications. Never use on acetate fabrics. Dwell 3–5 minutes.

3. Apply NSD to emulsify any remaining oil micro-droplets and remove solvent residue. The detergent surrounds oil particles and allows them to be flushed away. Rinse thoroughly with cool water. Dwell 5 minutes.
4. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to restore fiber to its natural state. Blot dry and allow to air dry. Dwell 1–2 minutes.
professionalProtocol.temperature: warm for rinsing — warm water helps emulsify residual oil; treat with solvent first before introducing water
warnings: Rubbing the stain — lipstick contains concentrated dye that spreads aggressively
Using water first — water doesn't dissolve wax or oil, and the dye is not water-soluble
Putting the stained garment in the dryer — melts wax and transfers lipstick to other clothes
Skipping the solvent step and going straight to OxiClean — oxygen bleach cannot reach dye still locked in the wax matrix

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain. Wait 5 minutes.
2. Brush off the powder with a clean white cloth.
3. Put a drop of Dawn dish soap on the stain. Blot it in.
4. Let it sit for 2–3 minutes. Rinse with cold water.
5. Repeat if needed. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Try a dry cleaning solvent (Carbona Stain Devil #5 or Goo Gone). If the stain survives two rounds of treatment, a professional cleaner can use concentrated agents and specialized equipment.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing pushes the stain deeper into the fabric.
Never add water to an oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, and you'll spread it.
Putting the stained garment in the dryer transfers the stain to other clothes.

neverDo: Put the stained garment in the dryer — melts wax and permanently transfers stain
Add water first — doesn't dissolve wax or oil, and spreads the dye
Rub the stain — lipstick dye spreads aggressively
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates most agents including bleach on whites. For colored cotton, test peroxide first. High heat can set protein stains. OxiClean is safe on cotton.

escalation.when: Long-wear or matte liquid lipstick with film-forming polymers doesn't respond to standard solvent
escalation.whatToTell: Lipstick stain on cotton, oil/wax/dye combination. Treated at home with dish soap. Please use dry-side solvent protocol with POG or amyl acetate.

contextQuestions: Is this regular lipstick, long-wear/matte lipstick, or lip gloss?
Is the stain fresh or has it been set by heat?
Has the garment been through the dryer?
Is the cotton white or colored?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Long-wear and matte liquid lipsticks contain additional film-forming polymers that may require a glycerin pre-treatment. Lip gloss is primarily oil and wax without as much dye. Dryer exposure melts wax into the fiber and makes removal very difficult. On colored cotton, test H₂O₂ on a hidden seam first.
